notes: Named as a campaign origin point alongside Phuket, but no dedicated Bangkok-specific incident (parallel to Phuket's Andaman-coast operation) was found

fact_label: Condo foreign-ownership quota applies without variation
notes: Same national 49% cap, confirmed applying to Bangkok without variation
Bumrungrad International ranked 96th in Newsweek's World's Best Hospitals 2026 — the only Thai hospital in the global top 100; BNH also in-city
15% improvement vs. prior year; materially below Chiang Mai's 188 μg/m³ March peak, a real problem here too but not an equivalent-severity one

value_raw: Large, includes significant non-Western migrant-labor population
notes: The city's own research explicitly warns against reading a "1.5 million" headline figure as "1.5 million Western expats" — it includes long-term expats, retirees, and a large Myanmar/Cambodia/Laos migrant-labor population; no isolated Western-resident-only count found
Bangkok's well-documented monsoon-flooding/land-subsidence risk hasn't been researched for this project — the same class of absence as Phuket's tsunami gap, just a different hazard mechanism
Chiang Mai and Phuket both got this treatment 2026-07-08; Bangkok's own figures still rest on the original 2026-07-07 sourcing
Sits between Phuket's (60.46, weakest) and Chiang Mai's (77.9, strongest)
~2.5x Chiang Mai's citywide average — the highest price-per-sqm of Thailand's three scored clusters, meaning the smallest floor area purchasable per dollar of any Thailand cluster researched
24-30% pricier than Chiang Mai on a like-for-like basis (per Chiang Mai's/Phuket's own cross-checks)
Roughly 1.3-2.5x the citywide average — floor area purchasable at typical entry-level relocation budgets in this tier is not realistically livable
A constant, megacity-scale friction, not a seasonal spike

WHO guideline is 5 μg/m³; world's most polluted city on multiple occasions, March 2026. Anchor-verification correction: same heading text carried over unchanged in the migration, but the file path didn't — this content now lives in Chiang Mai's own red-flags.md, not the national one
Still open — checked directly, this anchor is genuinely still national-scope and unmoved by the migration, no fix needed

Full range $900-2,500 depending on lifestyle; runs 24-30% cheaper than Bangkok on the like-for-like aggregator cross-check. Anchor-verification correction: old citation (Thailand/cost-of-living.md#chiang-mai) no longer resolves — the national file now covers Chiang Mai only as a bullet inside its cross-cluster comparison, not its own heading; the real figures live in Chiang Mai's own per-location file
Bangkok Hospital Chiang Mai is internationally accredited (JCI) with English-speaking staff — a genuine regional draw, not just an adequate backstop

Predates the pandemic-era nomad boom by over a decade — on paper, the deepest, most established remote-worker community infrastructure of Thailand's three clusters, arguably deeper than anywhere in this project except Antigua

value_raw: Twenty times more white faces than Thai
notes: A specific, quotable ratio claim underlying the crowded/curated critique above — held at Medium-or-lower confidence in scorecard.md's own reasoning for keeping Chiang Mai's Community & social fabric score at 4, not 5; not independently re-verified this pass

Chiang Mai-specific, distinct from the national blended 272.65 Mbps figure. Anchor-verification correction: the old citation's heading still exists nationally, but the national file's own text explicitly says location-specific speeds "live in its own overview.md, not repeated here" — the 184/68 figure was never actually at that anchor, only the blended national number is

fact_label: International hospital accreditation (JCI) depth — Bangkok Hospital Chiang Mai
value_raw: Gold Seal of Approval, 4 consecutive cycles, 2015-2027
notes: Not a one-time certification; specialty range covers orthopedics, cardiology, oncology, bariatrics, fertility, cosmetic surgery — the 36th hospital in the national Bangkok Hospital Group network (bangkokhospital.com, konkai.health)

#1 SE Asia, 19th globally — the strongest of Thailand's three scored clusters on this measure. Anchor-verification correction: old citation pointed at the national red-flags.md heading (which covers the 2.5-2.6/100,000 national homicide rate, not this location-specific index score) — the real figure lives in Chiang Mai's own red-flags.md under its own heading

≈$1,570–1,720/sqm at 32-36 THB/USD. Anchor-verification correction: old citation (Thailand/property.md#chiang-mai--the-only-cluster-with-real-depth) points at a heading that no longer exists — the national file's own heading was renamed to "Purchase-price data — now real for all three scored clusters" once Phuket/Bangkok were added; the citywide-average figure itself now lives in Chiang Mai's own per-location file

Multiple 2026 sources describe the scene as crowded, with sharply risen prices eroding its original draw, plus a pointed critique that the town reads as "curated for foreign spending rather than genuine local-culture immersion" — the same underlying population density as the countable-fact row above, read critically rather than positively

fact_label: Air-quality crisis comparable to Chiang Mai's
notes: An absence of finding, not a confirmed clean-air guarantee — no dedicated Phuket air-quality search was run

Roughly 2.4x the low tier's price per sqm — only a small studio is achievable at typical entry-level relocation budgets in this tier; a separate, unreconciled ~140,000 THB/sqm blended figure also surfaced and is flagged as unverified rather than picked
Not broken out by cluster
Numbeo and Expatistan land within ~2% of each other; Phuket runs 35-40% above Chiang Mai on the same cross-check

value_raw: In-city, internationally accredited (JCI), first on the island
notes: Bangkok Hospital Phuket — first hospital on the island to receive Joint Commission International (JCI) accreditation, 200 inpatient beds, 23+ specialty centers, serves 80,000-90,000 international patients
A hospitality/lifestyle-entrepreneurship economy (foreign-owned restaurants, dive shops, villas-run-as-businesses), a materially different shape than Chiang Mai's coworking/nomad hub
Some agents report buyer hesitancy and shift toward condos; others report no withdrawal at all

Phase 3, 500+ officers, 76 companies raided, ฿1.053B seized, 59 arrest warrants — sharper figures than the earlier ~29 companies/48 plots reported the prior week; named individual (Andrew, British) arrested at Sava Beach Villa
Weakest of Thailand's three scored clusters on this measure; concerns skew toward corruption perception and property crime, not violent crime
One first-hand account of six longtime expat friends all leaving, citing traffic as the deciding factor
The lowest price-per-sqm tier surveyed in Phuket — a real 1-2BR condo is achievable in this tier at typical entry-level relocation budgets

The single most notable absence in this table — Phuket's 2004 tsunami history is well-documented globally, and this project has genuinely detailed structural-hazard research for lower-profile risks elsewhere (Morocco's Taghazout earthquake/tsunami section), but none yet for Phuket itself

value_raw: None of Thailand's four routes offers a clean, income-level-consistent path from legally-resident to PR
notes: The Destination Thailand Visa (DTV) and Privilege don't clear the first hurdle at all (neither is a Non-Immigrant category); Non-Immigrant O-A/O-X and the Long-Term Resident (LTR) visa (on one of two conflicting readings) clear the 3-year-clock precondition, but neither route's own qualifying income/asset test satisfies any of the five substantive PR categories on its own — a general, route-level finding, kept fully neutral per this table's non-negotiable extraction rule, not a comparison to any specific person's numbers

Also requires a clean immigration record and application from outside Thailand via the e-Visa platform, which reportedly checks IP/GPS to enforce the "outside Thailand" rule. group_key/group_role wired 2026-07-14 - this fact was extracted 2026-07-07, before the companion-fact convention existed, and was never linked to its route; a real, sourced value that simply sat unlinked, same class as the P1 anchor-less-route finding.

fact_label: Destination Thailand Visa (DTV) converts to Thai PR
notes: Confirmed: the Destination Thailand Visa (DTV) sits outside the Non-Immigrant classification Thai PR requires; a confirmed structural dead end. Anchor precision improved this pass: re-pointed from the bare file citation to the verified Option 1 heading anchor

fact_label: Destination Thailand Visa (DTV) financial requirement (bank balance)
notes: Held for 3+ months prior to application; cryptocurrency and investment-portfolio balances explicitly not accepted (siam-legal.com, dtvvisathailand.com)

fact_label: Destination Thailand Visa (DTV) government application fee
notes: Base government fee is 10,000 THB; actual charge varies by issuing embassy/consulate (most 10,000-14,000 THB, some outliers higher). Non-refundable on rejection. Distinct from the 500,000 THB bank-balance threshold above (thaiembassy.com, siam-legal.com, royalthaiembassy.com, cross-checked 2026-07-15)

Passive income can supplement active-income documentation but cannot substitute for it — supplementary_only, not a flat rejection. Practically still a dead end for a purely-passive profile with no active-income documentation to supplement.

fact_label: Destination Thailand Visa (DTV) carries no health-insurance mandate
notes: Confirmed by absence — no insurance requirement stated anywhere in the Destination Thailand Visa (DTV)'s own documentation standards; the O-A/O-X compound gate above is route-specific, not universal

fact_label: Four-tier foreigner pricing schedule at public hospitals
value_raw: Thai base rate < ASEAN < working-expat < retiree/tourist (top tier)
notes: Ministry of Public Health tiering since 2019; retirees/tourists often pay ~50% above Thai pricing for the same service (phuketfamilyhealth.com, 2026) — public hospitals aren't closed to foreigners, but not at subsidized rates either

Explicitly checked and not found — "no active cartel-style organized-crime violence targeting foreign residents or tourism infrastructure," a real contrast with Mexico's Jalisco/Quintana Roo findings — a confirmed clean answer, not an unresearched gap

value_raw: No age requirement stated for any of the 4 categories
notes: The one route in this file with no confirmed age gate — the income/asset bar is the binding constraint instead

fact_label: Long-Term Resident (LTR) financial-security requirement accepts a savings/checking balance as an alternative to insurance
notes: Softer than O-A/O-X's insurance-only mandate — an applicant who can't clear an insurer's age ceiling can substitute a bank balance instead

fact_label: Long-Term Resident (LTR) financial security requirement (all 4 categories)
value_raw: USD 50,000+ health insurance, OR USD 100,000+ savings/checking balance
notes: Applies on top of each category's own income/asset test above — a cross-cutting AND-prerequisite, not an alternative qualifying tier, so it is deliberately NOT grouped under any one category's group_key (would export a bogus second 'tier' row per route).

fact_label: Long-Term Resident (LTR) Highly Skilled Professional — income requirement
notes: Reduced for advanced degrees/exceptional qualification; restricted to named sectors (automotive, electronics, digital tech, medical, others).

fact_label: Long-Term Resident (LTR) Highly-Skilled Professional converts to Thai PR
value_raw: disputed: Forbes & Partners (LTR is a Non-Immigrant category, 3 years satisfies the PR precondition) vs. Integrity Legal/PKF Thailand (LTR does not count at all — holder must cancel LTR, obtain a standard Non-Immigrant visa, and restart the 3-year clock from zero)
notes: Genuine, sourced conflict, kept as an explicit disagreement per direct instruction, not averaged away: Forbes & Partners (Thai immigration-law firm) holds the Long-Term Resident (LTR) visa is a formal Non-Immigrant category and that 3 consecutive years satisfies the PR precondition, same as O-A/O-X. Integrity Legal/legal.co.th and PKF Thailand hold LTR does not count at all. Both firms' own source pages were blocked by bot-verification on direct fetch — findings are via search-summary only. The two-firm dispute is about the LTR program's own Non-Immigrant classification, so every category inherits it identically; the dispute itself remains unresolved (open follow-up item, still pending).

fact_label: Long-Term Resident (LTR) Highly-Skilled Professional income-type requirement (passive)
notes: Asset- or employment-based tests, not passive-income routes at all — the source states this finding collectively for all three Long-Term Resident (LTR) non-pensioner categories; this row transports that collective statement, not new synthesis.

fact_label: Long-Term Resident (LTR) Wealthy Global Citizen — asset requirement
value_raw: USD 1,000,000+ total assets, including USD 500,000+ in specific Thai assets
notes: No age requirement; the previously-required USD 80,000 annual income floor has reportedly been removed as of this pass's sources.

notes: "Pension, rental, dividends" explicitly named as qualifying income. Genuinely blends pension-type and ordinary-passive-type income under one category, unlike Colombia's/Argentina's split Rentista-vs-Pensionado routes.

fact_label: Long-Term Resident (LTR) Work-from-Thailand Professional — income requirement
notes: Employed by a publicly listed company or a private firm with 3+ years' operation and USD 50M+ annual revenue — built for a W-2-style remote employee, not a solo/passive-income case.

Well below every Latin American candidate in this project. Anchor-verification correction: the heading itself gained a "(national baseline)" suffix since the table was first built (disambiguating it from Chiang Mai's own now-separate "Crime and homicide" heading, post-migration) — anchor updated to match

A structural mismatch independent of the financial numbers for anyone under 50. group_key/group_role wired 2026-07-14 - same pre-convention wiring gap as the Destination Thailand Visa (DTV) age-gate row above.

fact_label: O-A / O-X converts to Thai PR
value_raw: Yes (clears the 3-year Non-Immigrant precondition, but retirement isn't one of Thailand's 5 substantive PR categories — a retiree must separately qualify, most plausibly via Investment's ~$310,000/฿10M bar)
notes: Both are Non-Immigrant "O" visas, so 3 consecutive years satisfies the PR precondition — but retirement is not one of Thailand's five substantive PR-application categories, so a retiree would need to separately clear one of those five (most plausibly Investment's ฿10M/~$310,000 bar). The legal mechanics of the precondition itself are not in dispute (unlike LTR).

Ordinary pension/retirement/investment income explicitly accepted (embassy affidavit, security-deposit/bank-balance test) — no active-employment or freelance-contract documentation standard, the opposite shape from the Destination Thailand Visa (DTV)'s Workcation category. Genuinely blends pension-type and ordinary-passive-type income under one category, unlike Colombia's/Argentina's split routes.

value_raw: 800,000 THB (~$25,000) bank balance, OR 65,000 THB/month (~$2,000) income, OR a combination totaling 800,000 THB
notes: Balance can't drop below 400,000 THB during the visa year; mandatory health insurance (40,000 THB outpatient / 400,000 THB inpatient minimum, OIC-approved insurer)
Raised from the original 400,000 THB inpatient / 40,000 THB outpatient minimum (expatsinbangkok.com, pacificprime.com)

fact_label: O-A/O-X retirement visa — mandatory Office of Insurance Commission (OIC)-approved health insurance
notes: Required since 31 Oct 2019 (thaiwebsites.com); loosely/inconsistently enforced for years, now **strictly enforced as of 2025-2026** (pacificprime.com) — a real compound gate stacked against the age-ceiling rows above, specific to this route
Nearly 4x O-A's bar; security deposit held in a Thai bank account
US, UK, Canada, Australia, Germany, France, Italy, Netherlands, Norway, Denmark, Finland, Sweden, Switzerland, Japan — a categorical gate independent of the financial threshold

fact_label: Office of Insurance Commission (OIC)-approved insurer new-enrollment age ceiling (market range)
notes: Pacific Cross to 75 (renewable to 99); IMG Global to 74; Cigna states no upper limit for new enrollment (insurance-thailand.com) — a real, checked range, not assumed

fact_label: Office of Insurance Commission (OIC)-approved insurer renewal age ceiling (market range)
notes: Applies regardless of a given policy's own new-enrollment ceiling

fact_label: Office of Insurance Commission (OIC) co-payment rule (added cost pressure on older/frequent claimants)
notes: A real, dated policy change, not a narrative impression (insurance-thailand.com)

source_ref: [GAP — Thailand's own research is dog-specific only]
notes: Whether cat rabies-titer/quarantine requirements differ from the dog findings above has not been checked

fact_label: Pet import (dog) — rabies titer / quarantine requirement
value_raw: No blanket rabies-titer requirement; quarantine discretionary, not automatic
notes: Import Permit required in advance (60-day validity); vaccination 21+ days before departure against rabies/leptospirosis/distemper/hepatitis/parvovirus; a quarantine officer retains discretionary authority to detain any pet 30+ days if documentation is incomplete
50,000-200,000 THB for planned procedures; up to 800,000 THB for major surgery
~25,000-65,000 THB/year; local Thai policies for foreigners run cheaper but with more restrictions (thethaiger.com, alea.care)

Bronze (5yr) ฿650k (~$19k), Gold ฿900k (~$26.5k), Platinum (10yr) ฿1.5M (~$44k), Diamond (15yr) ฿2.5M (~$73.5k), Reserve (20yr) ฿5M (~$147k). Bronze is a promotional tier currently extended to September 2026 — confirm availability before relying on that price

fact_label: Privilege visa accepts passive income as qualifying proof
value_raw: N/A — no income or asset test of any kind, active or passive
notes: The only one of Thailand's four routes where the income-type-match question doesn't arise at all — the entire qualification is the one-time cash payment

The one route with no age gate and no income threshold to clear at all - value_raw normalized to '0' (confirmed no gate), same convention used elsewhere for a confirmed 'no adjustment' value. group_key/group_role wired 2026-07-14 - same pre-convention wiring gap as the Destination Thailand Visa (DTV)/O-A-O-X age-gate rows above.

fact_label: Privilege visa converts to Thai PR
notes: Confirmed, not just implied: not a Non-Immigrant visa category, time held doesn't count toward the 3-year clock. A holder who wants PR must separately obtain a standard Non-Immigrant visa (LTR, O-A/O-X, or employment/investment) and start the clock from there — buys stay-length and convenience, not a residency track

source_ref: [GAP — no insurance requirement found in this file's Privilege visa research]
notes: Honest absence-of-finding, not a confirmed clean bill — Option 4's own research states "no income or asset test of any kind" but doesn't address insurance specifically

value_raw: None — one-time cash payment only, no ongoing income/asset test
notes: Does not by itself confer a right to work — would need pairing with Destination Thailand Visa (DTV)-style remote-work legality if income is active rather than purely passive

fact_label: Public healthcare system (30-baht/UCS) has no eligibility route for most foreign residents
notes: Retirees, digital nomads, freelancers, and non-working spouses have no eligibility path into the public scheme at all; the sole exception is a valid Thai work-permit holder, who enrolls instead via the separate Social Security Scheme (eng.nhso.go.th, phuketfamilyhealth.com, 2024/2026)

fact_label: Referral pathway for complex/rare cases, Chiang Mai/Phuket → Bangkok
notes: Both regional flagship hospitals sit on the same Bangkok Hospital Group network as Bangkok's own hospitals, but actual referral frequency/practice wasn't found — feeds the system-capability component of each location's score without being assumed either way
Condominium Act B.E. 2522.
Willis Towers Watson 2026 Global Medical Trends report — insurance cost is a moving target, not fixed

fact_label: Retirement status alone satisfies none of Thailand's five substantive PR categories
notes: No O-A/O-X/Long-Term Resident (LTR)-Wealthy-Pensioner holder qualifies for PR on retirement status alone, regardless of years held — they'd need to separately clear one of the five categories above, most plausibly Investment, which sits far above any retirement-route income/asset bar

fact_label: Tenure mechanism — direct freehold, quota-capped (condo units)
notes: Legal on paper; once the 49% quota is reached, no further units in that building can be sold to foreigners.

value_raw: Yes (30-year lease, renewable by fresh negotiation)
notes: Materially weaker than Mexico's 50-year renewable fideicomiso or Guatemala's state land-reserve office (OCRET) lease — renewal is a fresh negotiation, not automatic.
National enforcement campaign has escalated (Phase 3, June 2026) rather than settled.
Up to 3yr imprisonment, ฿1M fine, deportation + blacklisting — applies to the foreign buyer, not just the Thai nominee.

fact_label: Thai PR — five substantive application categories
value_raw: Investment; Employment; Humanitarian/family; Expert; Extra Circumstances
notes: Investment: min ฿10,000,000 (~$310,000) in a Thai limited company, the stock market, or a state-issued security. Employment: ~฿80,000/month or ฿100,000/year in income tax paid for 2+ years, held alongside a Non-B work-permit visa. Humanitarian/family: marriage 2+ years to a Thai national, or a biological parent/child relationship. Expert: bachelor's degree plus specialized ability. Extra Circumstances: discretionary, "benefit to Thailand." Holding a qualifying visa for 3 years is only the precondition — an applicant must separately clear one of these five
A hard numerical cap independent of individual qualification — worth surfacing as its own fact, not folded into the five-category description above

fact_label: Thai PR precondition — 3 consecutive years on a qualifying Non-Immigrant visa
notes: Only a precondition, not the PR application itself — a structural fact load-bearing enough to belong in its own section rather than repeated per-route. Categories that count: Non-B (employment), Non-O (family-retirement), and the Long-Term Resident (LTR) visa on one of two conflicting readings (see LTR's converts_to_pr row above)

fact_label: Thai PR total fees on approval
notes: ฿100,000+ all-in for the approval-stage categories

fact_label: Thailand has four real, currently active visa/residency routes (index row)
value_raw: Destination Thailand Visa (DTV); Non-Immigrant O-A/O-X (retirement); Long-Term Resident (LTR) visa (4 sub-categories); Privilege/Elite
notes: Each route gets its own row-cluster below rather than being collapsed into one row per criterion

source_ref: [GAP — not researched anywhere in Thailand's files]
notes: A genuinely notable absence given Phuket's well-documented 2004 tsunami history — every other coastal/seismic candidate in this project (Morocco, Guatemala) has this category researched in real depth; Thailand currently has none of it

fact_label: Whether Long-Term Resident (LTR) Wealthy Global Citizen's USD 500,000+ Thai-asset holding satisfies the separate ฿10M PR Investment-category requirement
source_ref: [GAP — asset-type overlap not confirmed this pass]
notes: The two thresholds are in the same ballpark (USD 500k ≈ ฿16-18M, comfortably above ฿10M) but the type of asset each requires (LTR: broadly "Thai assets"; PR Investment category: specifically a Thai limited company, the stock market, or a state-issued security) hasn't been checked for overlap — named as an open question in visa-legal.md itself

fact_label: Two prime ministers removed by Constitutional Court in a 13-month window
value_raw: Srettha Thavisin (Pheu Thai) removed August 2024; Paetongtarn Shinawatra (also Pheu Thai) removed for an ethics violation August 29, 2025 — same removal mechanism used against PM Yingluck Shinawatra in 2014
notes: CNBC 2025-09-01. A real, recent, dated institutional-handover fact, not a distant-history point. PM Anutin Charnvirakul's Bhumjaithai Party then won a snap election Feb 8, 2026 and was reelected by parliamentary vote March 2026, with institutional/military alignment behind the government read by most analysts as reducing near-term instability risk

fact_label: Coup history and current risk read
value_raw: 12-13 successful military coups since 1932, most recently 2014; no actual coup in 12 years running; Defence Minister/Deputy PM Phumtham Wechayachai stated directly a coup "can never be completely ruled out"
notes: Council on Foreign Relations, East Asia Forum, 2025-2026. Multiple analysts read the Constitutional Court itself as historically aligned with the military/monarchy/traditional elite, retaining practical ability to dissolve parties and remove sitting PMs without a traditional coup needing to happen — the removal mechanism is live and recent (via the courts), the traditional military version specifically hasn't recurred in over a decade. Both facts true simultaneously

value_raw: Over 65% of voters backed drafting an entirely new constitution to replace the military-authored 2017 charter — outcome and timeline not yet resolved as of this research
notes: ConstitutionNet. An unprecedented popular challenge to the coup-era constitution — a real, live instance of the institutional rules themselves being actively renegotiated right now

fact_label: Baht currency volatility, with political uncertainty named as a contributing factor
notes: nationthailand.com, tradingeconomics.com. Widening US-Thailand interest-rate expectations, high household debt, weak consumption, and — named explicitly in at least one source — political uncertainty as a contributing factor to the volatility itself, not purely an externally-driven story. Bank of Thailand held its policy rate at 1% through 2026, its lowest since late 2022. Net: no currency crisis, but real, cited, named political-uncertainty pressure on the currency

fact_label: Foreign-investment posture — genuinely two-track, not one uniform story
value_raw: Board of Investment (BOI)/corporate level: 2025 FDI value rose 66% YoY to a record ฿1.36 trillion, 2026 branded internally as "the golden year of Thai investment." Informal/individual level (nominee-ownership channel): escalating criminal enforcement, a 2026 proposal to replace forced sale with zero-compensation forfeiture
notes: Kreston Thailand, Bangkok Post, nationthailand.com. Same government running two genuinely different postures simultaneously — welcoming and increasingly generous toward Board of Investment (BOI)-registered corporate capital, actively hostile and escalating toward informal/individual foreign land and business ownership structures. Which track applies depends entirely on the vehicle a foreign investor actually uses, not on some single national mood — directly relevant to land-property-access findings on the nominee crackdown
